questions.question: What is 'fogging' and when should you use it?

options.text: Fogging means avoiding conflict by agreeing with everything someone says
Fogging means agreeing with the factual truth in someone's criticism without accepting their judgment—used to defuse attacks without defending
Fogging means confusing the other person so they forget what they were criticizing
Fogging is the same as people-pleasing

questions.explanation: Fogging is an advanced assertiveness technique where you agree with the grain of truth in criticism (e.g., 'You're right, I am prioritizing my own needs this time') without accepting the judgment or changing your behavior. It defuses the attack because you're not resisting or defending—their criticism can't land. It's NOT people-pleasing because you're not accepting shame or changing your boundary.

questions.question: What does 'broken record' technique involve, and why does it work?

options.text: Repeating the other person's words back to them

Calmly repeating your boundary using the exact same words every time they push back, without justifying, arguing, defending, or explaining (JADE)—it works because your consistency signals you won't be worn down
Ignoring the person completely until they give up
Changing your boundary each time to avoid conflict

questions.explanation: Broken record means repeating your boundary using the SAME words every time someone pushes back—without justifying, arguing, defending, or explaining (JADE). Example: 'I'm not able to lend money right now.' (repeat 3-4 times). It works because you're not giving them new material to argue against, and your consistency shows you won't be worn down.

questions.question: What is a 'meta-statement' and when should you use it?

options.text: A meta-statement is a philosophical observation about life
A meta-statement names the relational pattern happening in the interaction (e.g., 'I notice whenever I bring up a concern, you criticize me')—use it when you're stuck in a repetitive cycle
A meta-statement is when you summarize what the other person said
A meta-statement is a way to avoid the topic being discussed

questions.explanation: A meta-statement steps back from the content of the argument and names the relational pattern itself (e.g., 'I've noticed that every discussion about this topic becomes a fight'). Use it when you're stuck in a repetitive, unproductive cycle. It shifts from 'what we're arguing about' to 'how we're relating'—and reveals whether the other person is willing to change the dynamic.

questions.question: What are signs that a relationship may be 'structurally unsustainable' (beyond what communication skills can fix)?

options.text: You had one argument last week

You've set clear boundaries repeatedly, and they consistently violate them; they respond to boundaries with punishment or escalation; they refuse to acknowledge impact; they show no willingness to change
You disagree about politics or preferences
The relationship requires any effort at all

questions.explanation: A relationship is structurally unsustainable when: you've set boundaries repeatedly and they're consistently violated; boundaries are met with punishment or escalation; the person refuses to acknowledge impact; there's no willingness to change; or the pattern involves coercive control, personality pathology, or fundamental incompatibility. Communication skills improve process, not underlying respect or goodwill.

questions.question: What does research show about communication skills and long-term relationship satisfaction?

options.text: Better communication guarantees relationship happiness

Communication skills improve relationship PROCESS (how you interact) but don't resolve relationships lacking mutual respect, goodwill, or values alignment—longitudinal studies show communication alone doesn't predict long-term satisfaction
Communication skills don't matter at all in relationships
Communication training fixes all relationship problems

questions.explanation: Research shows communication skills improve HOW relationships function (process)—reduced escalation, increased clarity. However, longitudinal studies show communication training alone doesn't predict long-term satisfaction when deeper issues (incompatibility, lack of respect, unwillingness to change) exist. Communication matters, but it can't fix everything.
